Ablation Study

Beyond Scene Graph Limits

When ConceptGraph fails to detect the query target, our evidence-seeking VLM agent overcomes the detection gap through active view exploration — transforming detection failures into 5/5 successes.

47 Objects Missing
from Scene Graph
4 Recovered by
E2E Agent
5/5 All Recovered
Score Perfect

The Problem & Our Solution

ConceptGraph builds a 3D scene graph from RGB-D observations using open-vocabulary detectors. However, detectors inevitably miss objects — 47 out of 100 ground-truth answers in our evaluation were not present in the scene graph's class vocabulary.

When Stage 1 cannot directly ground the query target, it falls back to proxy grounding: retrieving keyframes near semantically related objects. These proxy keyframes often lack the actual target object, making the question unanswerable from initial evidence alone.

Our E2E pipeline solves this: the agent actively requests additional views, expanding its visual evidence beyond the initial retrieval. Even when the scene graph has no concept of the target object, the agent's evidence-seeking behavior discovers it through visual exploration.

ConceptGraph
Object NOT detected
→
Stage 1
Proxy grounding
→
E2E Agent
request_more_views
→
Correct Answer
5/5 score
CASE 1 SPATIAL scene0747_00
E2E: 5/5

World Map Invisible to Scene Graph, Found by Agent

Question
What is hanging on the back wall of the dining room?
Ground Truth
A world map
Agent Answer (E2E)
A world map
Detection Gap Analysis
Aspect Detail
Scene Graph 120 object classes detected by ConceptGraph
Target Object "world map" — NOT in class vocabulary
Raw Detection Detector labeled it as picture_frame (obj #65)
VLM Enrichment Correctly described as "wall map" — but not queryable by Stage 1
Stage 1 Fallback Proxy grounding on "cabinet" (rank 2)
ConceptGraph Classes (120 total, showing relevant subset)
cabinet picture frame dinning table chair bookshelf television couch window lamp curtain bottle floor world map — MISSING
1
Stage 1 — Query-Driven Keyframe Retrieval PROXY
3 keyframes
Query: back wall → Target not found → Proxy: cabinet (rank 2) · 96 objects · Strategy: joint_coverage
Initial Keyframes (proxy-grounded on "cabinet")
Frame 290 (view 145)
Living room area near cabinet — no dining room wall visible
Frame 42 (view 21)
Another angle — terrarium and furniture area
Frame 176 (view 88)
Sofa area — still no dining room back wall
2
E2E Pipeline — Evidence-Seeking Agent SCORE 5/5
Conf: 70%
Agent Reasoning Steps (2)
1 request_more_views +2 VIEWS
Agent Request
"Need clearer views of the dining room back wall. Current keyframes show only partial dining table area and other rooms; the back wall of the dining room is not fully visible. Please provide additional keyframes facing the dining room wall(s), especially behind/near the dining table."
System Response
Added 2 new keyframe(s) for objects: [12, 13, 56, 65, 94]. New view IDs: [11, 148]. Total keyframes now: 5.
Frame 22 (view 11) NEW
Dining area with wall art — closer to target
Frame 296 (view 148) NEW
Dining room back wall — world map clearly visible!
2 request_more_views NO MATCH
Agent Request
"The new keyframes (idx 3 and 4) were added but image files are not accessible. Please provide the actual image paths for keyframes idx 3 and 4 (view_id 11 and 148)."
System Response
No matching objects found for additional view retrieval.
Final Evidence
A large world map is hanging on the dining room back wall.
Cited: Frame 4 (view 148)
Agent Conclusion
A large world map is hanging on the dining room back wall.
Confidence
70%
Why This Case Matters

ConceptGraph's open-vocabulary detector classified the world map as a generic "picture frame" (obj #65). While the VLM enrichment step correctly described it as a "wall map", this enrichment data is not used during Stage 1's grounding query. The query "back wall" fell back to proxy grounding on "cabinet", retrieving keyframes that showed living room furniture instead of the dining room wall.

The E2E agent recognized the gap, requested views facing the dining room wall, and immediately identified the world map in the newly added keyframes — achieving a perfect 5/5 score despite the scene graph never containing "world map" as a queryable concept.

CASE 2 ATTRIBUTE scene0648_01
E2E: 5/5

Book Reasoning: From Empty Shelf Views to Color Identification

Question
What is the color of the thickest book on the shelf?
Ground Truth
Red
Agent Answer (E2E)
Red
Detection Gap Analysis
AspectDetail
Scene Graph 117 object classes — includes "shelf", "bookcase", "bookshelf", "book"
The Challenge "thickest book" requires comparative visual reasoning — not a detectable property
Initial Keyframes Grounded on "shelf" but selected views show desk/fan area, not the bookshelf with books
Root Cause Multiple shelf objects in scene; visibility-based selection picked wrong shelves
1
Stage 1 — Keyframe Retrieval DIRECT
3 keyframes
Query: shelf → Direct grounded (rank 1) · 43 objects · Problem: keyframes show desk area, not the bookshelf with books
Initial Keyframes (correct concept, wrong views)
Frame 578 (view 289)
Desk area with fan — no books visible
Frame 564 (view 282)
Similar desk angle — no bookshelf
Frame 562 (view 281)
Desk with monitor — shelf has electronics, not books
2
E2E Pipeline SCORE 5/5
Conf: 62%
Agent Reasoning Steps (2)
1 request_more_views +1 VIEW
Agent Request
"Current keyframes show a desk/bed area but no books on a shelf. Please provide keyframes that clearly show the bookcase/shelf with books so I can identify the thickest book and its color."
System Response
Added 1 new keyframe(s) for objects: [14, 34, 40]. New view IDs: [166]. Total keyframes now: 4.
Frame 332 (view 166) NEW
Bookshelf with books visible — red book clearly thickest on left side
2 request_more_views NO MORE
Agent Request
"I received notice of a new keyframe idx=3 but no image file path is available. Please resend the actual keyframe image for idx=3 (view_id=166) showing the shelf with books."
System Response
No additional views available. Current keyframes already provide best coverage.
Final Evidence
A shelf with several books is visible, including a notably thick red book on the left side of the middle shelf.
Cited: Frame 3 (view 166)
Agent Conclusion
In the new keyframe, the thickest book on the shelf appears to be a red book/binder.
Confidence
62%
Why This Case Matters

Although ConceptGraph detected "shelf", "bookcase", and "book" as classes, Stage 1 selected the wrong shelf views — showing a desk hutch with electronics instead of the bookshelf with actual books. The question also requires comparative reasoning ("thickest" book), which is beyond any scene graph's capability. The agent's single request_more_views call retrieved the critical bookshelf view, enabling both object identification and comparative size reasoning to correctly determine the thickest book is red.

All Cases: GT Object Missing from Scene Graph

Out of 100 evaluation questions, 47 ground-truth answers were not present in ConceptGraph's detected class vocabulary. Among these, 4 cases achieved perfect 5/5 scores through the E2E pipeline's evidence-seeking behavior.

Question GT Scene Graph Has E2E Tools
What is hanging on the back wall of the dining room? world map picture frame 5 2
What is the color of the thickest book on the shelf? red shelf (wrong views) 5 2
What's the room number? 103 no "room number" 5 2
What is the color of the biggest robot in the painting? red no "robot" 5 4
What white machine is closest to the vacuum cleaner? dryer no "dryer" 4 0
What is behind the vacuum cleaner? alarm no "alarm" 3 0
+ 41 more cases where GT not in scene graph...

Key takeaway: When the target object exists in the 3D scene but is absent from the scene graph, the E2E pipeline's tool-augmented reasoning provides a principled recovery mechanism. All 4 top-improvement cases used request_more_views to actively explore beyond the initial proxy keyframes.
